skill.name: nano-banana-2
skill.description: Generate and edit images with Google Gemini 3.1 Flash Image Preview through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Runs Nano Banana 2 image generation and editing through inference.sh.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command and network patterns. Most shell-execution and weak-cryptography detections are false positives from markdown examples, table text, or checksum documentation, but the Quick Start confirms a high-risk curl-to-shell install command. The skill also sends prompts and image inputs to an external inference.sh hosted model, so publication should require review or safer installation guidance.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The Quick Start recommends running curl from https://cli.inference.sh directly into sh before login. This is a real supply-chain risk because the downloaded script executes immediately without local inspection.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact command is present in a bash example and uses a remote script piped to sh. The surrounding text claims checksum verification, but the install command still executes remote code directly.

medium_findings.title: External Hosted Model Receives User Prompts And Images
medium_findings.description: The documented commands send prompts and optional image URLs or files to the inference.sh app google/gemini-3-1-flash-image-preview. This is expected behavior for the skill, but users should treat prompts and images as data shared with an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CLI and SDK examples clearly invoke an external inference.sh application. This is a privacy and data handling concern, although it matches the stated purpose of image generation.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misclassified As Ruby Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick findings correspond to markdown code fences, inline code spans, and documented CLI examples. No Ruby code or dynamic backtick execution is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows fenced bash and python documentation rather than Ruby source. The detected pattern is a scanner artifact caused by markdown syntax.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Checksum Or Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography detections map to product text, markdown table rows, and a note that SHA-256 checksums are verified. No weak hash algorithm or cryptographic implementation is used by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only checksum reference is SHA-256 verification, which is not weak cryptography. Other flagged lines are headings or output field names, not crypto code.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs Are Documentation And Example Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, the CLI installer, checksum files, and an example image URL. These are expected for this skill, but they confirm external network reliance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in documentation context and are not hidden or obfuscated. The risk is dependency on external domains rather than evidence of malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Pipe To Shell Install Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The install command downloads a script from an external domain and pipes it directly to sh. Marketplace review should require a safer manual install path or a warning before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a direct match for the dangerous install pattern and the command is meant for users to run. There is no evidence of confirmed malware, so the risk is high rather than critical.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked CLI Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples run infsh commands against a hosted model and optional Google Search grounding. This is functional behavior, but it means user content can leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network dependency is explicit in the model and service names. The evidence supports a data-sharing concern, not hidden exfiltration.

content.user_title: Generate Gemini Images With Nano Banana 2
content.value_statement: Teams need fast image drafts and edits without switching tools. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users prompt patterns for running Gemini image generation through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to run text-to-image generation with the inference.sh CLI.
Documents image editing with input images and a natural language prompt.
Lists options for image count, aspect ratio, resolution, and output format.
Includes examples for Google Search grounding in visual prompts.
Provides Python SDK examples for standard and streamed model runs.
Links to inference.sh documentation for app execution, streaming, and files.
limitations: Requires an installed and authenticated inference.sh CLI or SDK client.
Sends prompts and image inputs to an external hosted inference service.
Does not include local model weights or offline image generation.
The Quick Start install command uses a high-risk curl-to-shell pattern.

use_cases.title: Create campaign concept images
use_cases.description: Generate multiple visual directions for ads, landing pages, or social posts from a short creative brief.

use_cases.title: Edit reference images with prompts
use_cases.description: Apply requested visual changes to supplied images, such as adding scene elements or changing mood.

use_cases.title: Prototype image workflows in code
use_cases.description: Use the Python SDK examples to test model calls, streaming updates, and output handling.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a simple image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Nano Banana 2 to generate one square image of [subject]. Use [style], [lighting], and [mood].

prompt_templates.title: Create multiple variations
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate four variations of [concept] for [audience]. Keep the composition consistent and vary color, materials, and background.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an input image
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit the provided image by changing [specific element]. Preserve the original subject, camera angle, and main composition.

prompt_templates.title: Ground a current visual brief
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image based on current information about [topic]. Enable Google Search grounding and show the result as [visual format].

output_examples.input: Prompt for a photorealistic banana floating above Earth with studio lighting.
output_examples.output: The skill returns generated image results and may include a short model description of the scene.

output_examples.input: Request to add a rainbow to a supplied landscape image.
output_examples.output: The skill returns edited image results that preserve the landscape while adding the requested sky element.

output_examples.input: Prompt for four minimalist coffee shop logo concepts.
output_examples.output: The skill returns multiple image variations for review and selection.

best_practices: Inspect installer scripts manually or use documented checksum verification before running them.
Write prompts with subject, style, lighting, composition, and output size requirements.
Avoid sending confidential images, private documents, or sensitive personal data to hosted models.
anti_patterns: Do not run the curl-to-shell installer in restricted or production environments without review.
Do not use vague prompts when specific visual direction is required.
Do not assume generated images are cleared for trademark, privacy, or policy-sensitive use.

faq.question: What does this skill help users do?
faq.answer: It helps users run Gemini image generation and image editing through the inference.sh CLI or Python SDK.

faq.question: Does it generate images locally?
faq.answer: No. The examples call a hosted inference.sh app, so prompts and images are processed by an external service.

faq.question: Can it edit existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents input images for editing prompts, with support for common web image formats.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The Quick Start recommends piping a remote installer directly to sh, which can execute unreviewed code.

faq.question: What tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should users check before use?
faq.answer: Users should verify the CLI source, review data handling policies, and avoid sensitive prompts or images.
